title: Carrot and walnut cake
subtitle: Moist spiced cake with grated carrot and walnuts, finished with cream cheese frosting.
excerpt: Carrot and walnut cake uses oil rather than butter for an unusually moist crumb that keeps well for several days. Grated carrot adds moisture and a slight sweetness; walnuts add crunch. The frosting is cream cheese beaten with icing sugar: cool and tangy against the warm spice of the cake.
sourceNotes: Carrot cake has roots in medieval European baking, when carrots were used as a sweetener due to their high sugar content. The modern cream cheese frosted version became popular in the United States during the 1960s and spread to Britain in the 1970s. Delia Smith included a version in her 1978 cookery books that was widely influential. The oil-based sponge method produces a longer shelf life than butter; the cream cheese frosting became the canonical accompaniment. This recipe follows the standard British cream cheese frosted version.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted cake layers. Wrap each in cling film and freeze for up to two months. Defrost fully before frosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake layers can be baked a day ahead and stored wrapped at room temperature. The frosted cake keeps in the fridge for three days; remove 30 minutes before serving.

recipeTools.notes: Two 20 cm sandwich tins, greased and lined.
For the frosting. A wooden spoon works but takes more effort.

content.text: Carrot cake keeps better than most because oil stays liquid at room temperature, where butter sets firm. Three days after baking, a well-made carrot cake is still moist where a butter sponge would have dried out. The grated carrot contributes moisture and a mild sweetness rather than a strong vegetable flavour; two teaspoons of mixed spice gives the warmth.
The frosting is cream cheese and icing sugar: two ingredients, mixed cold. It sets firm enough to slice cleanly but stays creamy rather than stiff. Do not whip it too much or it can turn grainy.

items.prepNote: medium, about 250 g total, peeled and coarsely grated
roughly chopped, plus a few halves to decorate

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease two 20 cm sandwich tins and line the bases with baking paper.
Whisk {{sunflower-oil}}, {{light-brown-sugar}}, and {{eggs}} together in a large bowl until smooth and slightly thickened.
Sift in {{self-raising-flour}} and {{mixed-spice}} and stir briefly. Add {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} and mix until no dry flour remains. Fold in {{carrot}} and {{walnuts}}.

Divide the batter evenly between the two tins. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the surface springs back when lightly pressed. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn onto a rack.
Beat {{cream-cheese}} briefly with a wooden spoon or spatula to loosen it. Sift in {{icing-sugar}} and mix until smooth and thick. Do not over-beat or it will become runny.

When both cake layers are completely cool, place one on a board or plate and spread with half the frosting. Top with the second layer. Spread the remaining frosting over the top. Arrange a few walnut halves on top to finish.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Most carrot cake problems involve the frosting.

items.cause: Cream cheese at room temperature, or over-beaten.
items.fix: Use full-fat cream cheese cold from the fridge and mix by hand rather than with an electric mixer. If it has become runny, chill it for 30 minutes and beat gently again.

items.symptom: Cake layers domed in the centre, hard to stack level.
items.cause: Batter distributed unevenly in the tins, or oven hot spot.
items.fix: Level off domed layers with a serrated knife. It is worth checking your oven temperature with an oven thermometer.
